-lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"<!-- generated by /setup-merge-gate v1; do not edit by hand -->\\n# Merge gate — quick reference\\n\\nThis project runs the **merge-gate** CI workflow on every pull\\nrequest. The gate is the pre-merge phase of the harness; the full\\noperations playbook (canary suite, soft→hard promotion, validator\\ndisagreement runbook, roll-back) lives at\\n[`docs/merge-gate-operations.md`](merge-gate-operations.md).\\n\\n## Blocking rule\\n\\n`critical` or `high` severity Codex findings classified `uphold` by\\nthe Claude validator block merge. `medium` / `low` findings are\\ncommented but never block. See ADR-0005 for why this MVP runs the\\nClaude validator only (Gemini concordance is deferred pending\\nsoft-mode measurement data).\\n\\n## Severity matrix\\n\\n| Severity | Validator `uphold` | Validator `dismiss` (cited) | Validator `unsure` |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| critical | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\\n| high     | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\\n| medium   | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\\n| low      | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\\n\\n## Bypass label\\n\\nApply the `merge-gate-bypass` label to a PR to skip the gate. Bypass\\nis the **audited bypass lane** — one human (not the PR author) must\\napply the label, leave a PR comment with the reason, and add an\\nentry to the plan repo's bypass log within one business day. See\\noperations playbook §4 for the full bypass policy.\\n\\n## Soft → Hard promotion\\n\\nThe gate ships in **soft mode** (`harness.toml`\\n`[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \\\"true\\\"`): runs, reports, never\\nblocks. Flip to hard mode after meeting the quantitative gates in\\noperations playbook §2 (N ≥ 10 PRs, FPR ≤ 1, human sign-off) **and**\\nenabling the branch-protection rule on the check\\n`merge-gate / codex-review`.\\n\\n## Local re-installation\\n\\nThe skill `/setup-merge-gate` is idempotent. Re-running it shows a\\ndiff for the `[merge-gate]` section of `harness.toml` and prompts\\nonce; vendored agent and skill files under `.claude/` are overwritten\\nunconditionally. `--uninstall` removes the workflow, the\\n`[merge-gate]` section, the vendored copies, and this document (only\\nif its first-line marker is intact).\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/CONTEXT.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/CONTEXT.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Engine\\n\\nThe runtime that, given a Position and a Player choice, returns a Move. Loads the Base model and all Roster Adapters at startup and selects an Adapter per request.\\n\\n## Language\\n\\n**Inference Engine**:\\nThe runtime module wrapping the Base model and all Roster Adapters. Exposes `(Position, adapter_id, decoding_mode) → Move (+ optional policy distribution)`. Handles legality masking, decoding-mode dispatch, and per-request Adapter selection. Distinct from the Web API layer that calls it.\\n_Avoid_: bare \\\"model\\\" (overloaded — see Training CONTEXT for **Base model** vs **Adapter**)\\n\\n**Decoding mode**:\\nHow a Move is chosen from the policy logits over legal Moves. Two modes:\\n- **Greedy** — deterministic argmax over the masked logits. Used by the Evaluation Harness for the top-1 Move-match metric.\\n- **Sample** — temperature-scaled softmax sampling (default temperature 0.7). Used in live gameplay so repeating an opening does not produce identical Games.\\n\\nOrthogonal to Player choice (any Adapter can be played in either mode) and to legality (mask applies in both).\\n\\n**Legality mask**:\\nThe boolean tensor over the 1968-action UCI space applied before decoding to set illegal-Move logits to -∞. Computed in two stages:\\n1. **Rule legality** — `python-chess` `board.legal_moves` from the FEN. Covers all chess rules whose verdict depends only on the current Position (piece movement, check, pin, en-passant, castling rights encoded in FEN).\\n2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's \\\"same Position\\\" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).\\n\\nThe 50-move-rule clock lives in the FEN and is already handled by stage 1. 5-fold and 75-move (automatic) are detected client-side as game-over events; the Inference Engine itself does not refuse to move once they occur.\\n\\n_Avoid_: \\\"legal moves filter\\\" — **mask** is the canonical term.\\n\\n**Policy distribution**:\\nThe post-mask, post-softmax distribution over the 1968 UCI actions for a given Position+Adapter+decoding-mode. Returned optionally for the comparison panel and the Evaluation Harness.\\n\\n## Implementation notes\\n\\nNot domain language, but load-bearing v1 decisions:\\n\\n- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.\\n- Adapter selection is **in-place per request**: the worker activates the requested Adapter, runs the forward pass, returns the Move, then accepts the next request. Two simultaneous requests for different Adapters are safe because they cannot overlap on the GPU (the lock guarantees no interleaved state mutation).\\n- The server is **stateless per request**. Game state lives in the browser (`chess.js`). The same Position with the same Adapter under greedy decoding returns the same Move every time; under sampling, varied.\\n- Throughput ceiling ≈ 7–10 moves/sec on the 3080. Practical concurrent active-user limit ≈ 25 before PRD User Story #5's \\\"couple of seconds\\\" budget starts breaking; beyond ~35 the request queue grows unboundedly. v1 has no rate limiting — this cliff is accepted as the scale of a portfolio demo. Batched inference, multi-GPU, and explicit backpressure are deliberate v2 levers (see PRD Out of Scope).\\n\\n## Relationships\\n\\n- **Training → Engine**: The Inference Engine consumes two artifact families produced by Training — one Base model checkpoint and one Adapter file per Roster Player. Engine never re-trains or modifies these artifacts.\\n- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked \\\"is the Session over?\\\", only \\\"given this Position and history, what is your Move?\\\". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.\\n- **Engine ⊥ Stockfish**: The Engine is not in the Stockfish comparison path. Stockfish runs client-side via `stockfish.wasm`. Engine and Stockfish are independent move sources that the Web context renders side by side.\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate-operations.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate-operations.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"<!-- generated by /setup-merge-gate v1; do not edit by hand -->\\n\\n# Merge gate — operations playbook\\n\\nThis document is the human-facing playbook for the **Merge gate** —\\nthe **Gate** for the pre-merge phase of the **Harness** (see\\n`CONTEXT-MAP.md`). The gate runs Codex's adversarial review as the\\nprimary reviewer and a Claude validator (MVP) that classifies each\\nfinding against project context before the gate decides to block.\\nThe MVP runs a single Claude validator per\\n[ADR-0005](adr/0005-claude-only-validator-mvp-gemini-deferred.md);\\na second validator returns once issue #10's soft-mode measurement\\ndata is in.\\n\\nThe **Setup skill** (`/setup-merge-gate`) installs the gate's\\nartifacts; it is not itself a gate. This playbook describes how to\\n*operate* the installed gate.\\n\\nGlossary terms (`Harness`, `Gate`, `Audited bypass lane`,\\n`Oracle weakening`) are defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md` — do not redefine\\nthem here; link or quote.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. Operational phases at a glance\\n\\n| Phase | Branch protection | Gate behaviour | Typical use |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| **Soft** | Not required | Runs, posts findings, **never blocks** | Data-collection — every PR is a measurement |\\n| **Hard** | Required check | Blocks merge on `critical` or `high` ∩ `uphold` | Steady state — gate is trusted |\\n\\nMode is recorded in `harness.toml` under `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default`\\n(string boolean: `\\\"true\\\"` = soft, `\\\"false\\\"` = hard). The flip from\\n`\\\"true\\\"` to `\\\"false\\\"` is HITL and is governed by §2 below.\\n\\nThe gate's check name is stable — `merge-gate / codex-review` — so\\nbranch-protection references do not need updating across mode flips.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Soft → Hard promotion checklist\\n\\nThe flip from `soft` to `hard` is the single moment the gate's\\nauthority changes. Treat it as a release, not a tweak. All items\\nmust be checked, and the human sign-off line must be filled in.\\n\\n### Quantitative gates\\n\\n- [ ] **N ≥ 10 non-canary PRs** measured under soft mode (canary\\n      PRs are excluded — they are deliberate defect injections, not\\n      organic traffic).\\n- [ ] **False-positive rate ≤ 1** across the N=10 set, where a false\\n      positive is *validator said block, human said \\\"I would not have\\n      wanted this blocked\\\"*.\\n- [ ] **False-negative rate recorded** — *validator said pass, human\\n      said \\\"I would have wanted this blocked\\\"*. A high FNR is **not**\\n      a blocker for the soft→hard flip (the gate's purpose is to\\n      cut false negatives over time), but it must be written down so\\n      the next iteration can target it.\\n\\n### Data layout\\n\\nEach PR's soft-mode data lives in the project's plan repo under\\n`.scratch/merge-gate/runs/<pr-number>.md` and records:\\n\\n- Codex JSON output (artefact link or inline)\\n- Claude verdict\\n- Final decision the gate would have made\\n- Human verdict: would I have wanted this blocked? (yes / no / unsure)\\n- One-line rationale if validator and human diverged\\n\\nA second validator's verdict slot will be added to this layout when\\nissue #10's measurement motivates re-introducing it.\\n\\n### Qualitative review\\n\\n- [ ] Skim every entry. Look for *patterns* in the false positives\\n      (e.g. \\\"Codex keeps flagging our intentional retry-without-cap\\n      because AGENTS.md does not state the upstream timeout\\n      contract\\\") rather than treating each as isolated noise. A\\n      pattern is a signal to tighten the validator's project\\n      references (§5), not to lower the bar.\\n- [ ] Confirm the **Audited bypass lane** was never silently used in\\n      this window — every `merge-gate-bypass` label application\\n      should appear in §4's audit log.\\n\\n### Human sign-off\\n\\n> Signed off by: `<name>`\\n> Date: `<YYYY-MM-DD>`\\n> N = `<count>` PRs · FPR = `<count>/<N>`\\n> Flipping `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default` from `\\\"true\\\"` to `\\\"false\\\"` and enabling the\\n> branch-protection rule on `merge-gate / codex-review`.\\n\\n### After the flip\\n\\n- Update `harness.toml`: `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \\\"false\\\"`.\\n- Enable the branch-protection rule (the `/setup-merge-gate` install\\n  output printed the exact rule text — keep it in the install log).\\n- Announce the flip in whatever channel the team uses; the first\\n  organic block under hard mode is the moment people learn the gate\\n  is real.\\n\\nIf any quantitative gate is unmet: **do not flip**. Open or update\\nthe soft→hard tracking issue with what is missing and which upstream\\nissue is the fix path (tighten validator prompt, expand AGENTS.md\\ncontext, etc.).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. Canary-PR guide\\n\\nCanary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway\\nbranches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known\\nrisk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a\\nknown answer key.\\n\\n### What to canary\\n\\nAt minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt\\nprioritises:\\n\\n| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |\\n\\n`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*\\nunder hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but\\ndoes not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always\\nnon-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.\\n\\n### Operating canaries\\n\\n- Each canary's brief lives at\\n  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,\\n  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.\\n- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.\\n- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the\\n  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).\\n- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.\\n\\n### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary\\n\\nA \\\"Codex false positive\\\" on a canary means Codex flagged something\\nthe brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:\\n\\n1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem\\n   unrelated to the injection. Record it as a found bug in the\\n   canary brief — that is a useful side effect, not noise.\\n2. **Context starvation.** Codex flagged code that looks risky in\\n   isolation but is safe given project conventions that AGENTS.md\\n   does not yet spell out. This is the validator's job to dismiss\\n   with a citation (γ Strict — see §5). If the validator also fails\\n   to dismiss, treat it as a *project-context gap*: open an issue per\\n   §4 tagged `merge-gate-fp` with a pointer to the AGENTS.md /\\n   `CONTEXT-MAP.md` text that should have made the dismissal possible.\\n\\n### Interpreting a missed canary\\n\\nIf a canary's expected severity is `critical` but Codex returns\\n`medium`, or returns nothing, record it in the brief with a short\\nhypothesis (e.g. \\\"Codex missed the rollback case — likely because\\nthe diff did not include the migration file\\\"). Missed canaries are\\nthe input for tightening the canary suite and/or the Codex prompt\\nin a later iteration — they are not blockers for soft→hard.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Audited bypass lane — the `merge-gate-bypass` label\\n\\nThe merge gate provides an **Audited bypass lane** in the sense\\ndefined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md`: an acknowledged, recorded way to pass the\\ngate without satisfying it. The lane exists so emergencies are not\\nforced through the silent-circumvention door. It is **not** a\\nshortcut for routine merges.\\n\\n### When the label is allowed\\n\\n- **Production incident hotfix** where the cost of waiting for the\\n  gate exceeds the cost of a missed finding, and the fix is small\\n  enough for a human to review end-to-end.\\n- **Confirmed Codex false positive** that the validator could not\\n  dismiss with a citation, where the team has already opened a\\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue per §5 and is unwilling to block the PR on\\n  the fix.\\n- **Infrastructure / docs-only PR** that should have hit the\\n  `docs-only` path glob but did not (treat the missed glob as a\\n  bug — fix the workflow's glob in a follow-up).\\n\\nThe label is **never** appropriate for:\\n\\n- \\\"The gate is slow today\\\" — that is a §7 cost/latency issue, file\\n  it there.\\n- \\\"I disagree with the finding\\\" without a written argument — open a\\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue and resolve through §5, not the bypass lane.\\n- Avoiding the validator `unsure` runbook in §6 — bypass is the\\n  lane of last resort, not the default for `unsure`.\\n\\n### Approval\\n\\n- **Single approver model.** One human who is not the PR author must\\n  apply the `merge-gate-bypass` label and leave a PR comment with\\n  the reason (one of the three categories above) and a link to any\\n  follow-up issue.\\n- For production-incident hotfixes, the approver is the on-call;\\n  for the other two categories, the approver is a project\\n  maintainer.\\n\\n### Audit trail\\n\\n- The label application is itself a GitHub event; that is the\\n  primary audit record.\\n- The approver's PR comment is the human-readable rationale.\\n- A follow-up entry is added to\\n  `.scratch/merge-gate/bypass-log.md` in the plan repo within one\\n  business day: PR link, approver, category, follow-up issue link.\\n\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"nl -ba src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py | sed -n '1,140p'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"nl -ba src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py | sed -n '1,140p'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"     1\\t\\\"\\\"\\\"FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.\\n     2\\t\\n     3\\tPhase 1 (random-move tracer): exposes POST /move that returns a uniformly\\n     4\\trandom legal move for the supplied FEN. The decision rejects the open-roster alternative because runtime training would require a job queue, GPU scheduling, failure handling, and abuse mitigation — infrastructure that would expand scope significantly past the (C)-grade usable-tool target and would also produce Adapter quality that varies per request (no human in the loop to validate hyperparameters or evaluation results before exposing the Adapter to users).\\n./docs/merge-gate-operations.md:326:| Codex token cost per gate run | TBD (#10 measurement) | same as soft | Adversarial-review prompt + diff payload |\\n./docs/merge-gate-operations.md:327:| Validator token cost per gate run | TBD (#10 measurement) | same as soft | One Claude validator pass over the Codex JSON (single-model MVP per ADR-0005) |\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:1:# Transformer over FEN tokens with policy head over fixed action space\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:3:We chose a Transformer that takes a single FEN string as tokenized text input and outputs logits over a fixed action space (~1968 UCI move encodings), rather than an LLM-style decoder over move-sequence tokens (α) or an AlphaZero-style ResNet over an 8×8×channels board tensor (β). This keeps the board state explicit (avoiding the implicit-board, long-context problem of (α)), makes illegal-move masking a one-line operation at the policy head, and aligns with LLM tooling for LoRA-based per-player adapters — strengths the other two options each lack.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:15:The input is a single FEN per request: no move history, no prior board states. Accepted because (a) at 9M params / 10GB VRAM the model cannot meaningfully learn long-horizon plans even if history were available, (b) adding history would multiply sequence length ~6–8× and force the model to shrink, and (c) per-Player archives are small (~10K Games), so same-FEN generalization across Games matters more than path-conditioning.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:27:**Encoder-only with a single readout vector → linear projection** is locked even though the rest is deferred: it bounds the forward graph (no causal mask over the FEN tokens, no autoregressive decoding loop) so downstream modules (Inference Engine, legality mask application, decoding modes) can be specified concretely now without waiting on sub-architecture The gate is the pre-merge phase of the harness; the full\noperations playbook (canary suite, soft→hard promotion, validator\ndisagreement runbook, roll-back) lives at\n[`docs/merge-gate-operations.md`](merge-gate-operations.md).\n\n## Blocking rule\n\n`critical` or `high` severity Codex findings classified `uphold` by\nthe Claude validator block merge. `medium` / `low` findings are\ncommented but never block. See ADR-0005 for why this MVP runs the\nClaude validator only (Gemini concordance is deferred pending\nsoft-mode measurement data).\n\n## Severity matrix\n\n| Severity | Validator `uphold` | Validator `dismiss` (cited) | Validator `unsure` |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| critical | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\n| high     | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\n| medium   | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\n| low      | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\n\n## Bypass label\n\nApply the `merge-gate-bypass` label to a PR to skip the gate. Bypass\nis the **audited bypass lane** — one human (not the PR author) must\napply the label, leave a PR comment with the reason, and add an\nentry to the plan repo's bypass log within one business day. See\noperations playbook §4 for the full bypass policy.\n\n## Soft → Hard promotion\n\nThe gate ships in **soft mode** (`harness.toml`\n`[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \"true\"`): runs, reports, never\nblocks. Flip to hard mode after meeting the quantitative gates in\noperations playbook §2 (N ≥ 10 PRs, FPR ≤ 1, human sign-off) **and**\nenabling the branch-protection rule on the check\n`merge-gate / codex-review`.\n\n## Local re-installation\n\nThe skill `/setup-merge-gate` is idempotent. Re-running it shows a\ndiff for the `[merge-gate]` section of `harness.toml` and prompts\nonce; vendored agent and skill files under `.claude/` are overwritten\nunconditionally. `--uninstall` removes the workflow, the\n`[merge-gate]` section, the vendored copies, and this document (only\nif its first-line marker is intact).\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

.scratch/merge-gate-canary/08-artefacts/run-1/codex-review.json:70:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_35","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/playlike_engine/api_test.py"","aggregated_output":""""HTTP tests for POST /move."""

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

from playlike_engine.api import app

STARTING_FEN = chess.STARTING_FEN
MID_GAME_FEN = "r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/2N2N2/PPPP1PPP/R1BQKB1R w KQkq - 4 4"
CHECKMATE_FEN = "rnb1kbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/6Pq/5P2/PPPPP2P/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 1 3"
INVALID_FEN_STR = "not a fen"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client() -> TestClient:
    return TestClient(app)


def test_starting_position_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": STARTING_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = response.json()
    assert set(body.keys()) == {"move_uci"}
    board = chess.Board(STARTING_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(body["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_mid_game_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": MID_GAME_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    board = chess.Board(MID_GAME_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(response.json()["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_invalid_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": INVALID_FEN_STR})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_checkmate_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": CHECKMATE_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_extra_field_rejected_by_strict_schema(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post(
        "/move",
        json={"fen": STARTING_FEN, "adapter_id": "magnus"},
    )
    assert response.status_code == 422
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

 from playlike_engine.random_move import NoLegalMovesError, pick_random_legal_move

     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

+    position: str
 
 
 class MoveResponse(BaseModel):
     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
     move_uci: str
 
 
 @app.post("/move", response_model=MoveResponse)
 def move(req: MoveRequest) -> MoveResponse:
     try:
-        board = chess.Board(req.fen)

     try:
         chosen = pick_random_legal_move(board)
     except NoLegalMovesError as exc:
         raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=str(exc)) from exc
     return MoveResponse(move_uci=chosen.uci())

docs/adr/0002-split-data-sources.md:11:For each Roster Player, the Chess.com Player Ingester writes its filtered Position shards under `data/snapshots/<player_id>/<YYYY-MM-DD>/` along with a `manifest.json` recording: fetch date, archive URL list, pre-filter and post-filter game counts, position count, the filter parameters that were applied (time-control set, rating-gap threshold, `rated == true`), and content hash. The Adapter and its Evaluation report are pinned to a specific snapshot ID. Re-fetching the archive at a later date produces a new snapshot, not an in-place update. Recording the filter parameters inside the manifest means a snapshot is reproducible end-to-end from the raw PubAPI responses, not just from already-filtered shards — useful if a filter is later revised and we want to re-derive an old snapshot's expected output.
docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:17:Known costs accepted for v1: path-conditioned style is not learnable — gambit follow-through, transposition-aware play, plan continuity from earlier in the Game. Threefold-repetition awareness must be added at the server's legality layer rather than learned by the model. AlphaZero feeds T=8 prior board-state planes precisely for these reasons; we are deliberately not following that pattern.
docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:19:Revisit trigger: if v1 evaluation or qualitative review shows clear path-conditioning deficits (transposition-zone errors, abandoned gambit themes), introduce a small history window (e.g. T=4 prior board states) and shrink the model to fit. Until then, path-dependence is out of scope.
docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.
docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:29:The baseline starting point is Ruoss et al.'s 9M configuration (DeepMind 2024). However, **deviation from that baseline requires an explicit pre-training architecture survey** — vanilla post-LN encoder transformer is no longer the 2026 default for small-scale models, and the gap between "DeepMind paper as-is" and the current small-transformer chassis (pre-LN, RoPE or learned absolute, SwiGLU/GLU MLPs, FlashAttention 2, bf16 mixed precision, possibly 2D board-aware positional structure) is large enough that locking the paper's exact shape without surveying 2024–2026 literature would leave free performance on the table. The survey is mandated as an acceptance criterion of issue 03 (real Base training), not deferred to v2 — the per-axis decisions and their rationale must be written down before any 9M training run is started, so that future ablation has a clean baseline and any strength miss has a small enough search space to diagnose.

src/engine/CONTEXT.md:8:The runtime module wrapping the Base model and all Roster Adapters. Exposes `(Position, adapter_id, decoding_mode) → Move (+ optional policy distribution)`. Handles legality masking, decoding-mode dispatch, and per-request Adapter selection. Distinct from the Web API layer that calls it.
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:21:2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's "same Position" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:34:- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:42:- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked "is the Session over?", only "given this Position and history, what is your Move?". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.
src/web/CONTEXT.md:8:One complete chess play instance in a single browser tab — from the starting Position to a Terminal state (or to a new-Session reset). Bounded by: one human player + one chosen Adapter (the Opponent), one selected human color (White or Black), one continuous game-state owned by the browser's `chess.js`. Distinct from Training's **Game** — a training **Game** is an archived sequence with one Player on each side; a Session is live, has only one Player (the Opponent), and is never persisted server-side. The same Position with the same Opponent and the same `move_history` returns the same Move under greedy decoding; under sampling, varied.
src/web/CONTEXT.md:12:The Roster **Player** selected for the current Session, exposed to the user as "Playing against: Magnus Carlsen". Realized in the wire as the `adapter_id` sent on every `/move` request. From the user's perspective the Opponent is "the Player I face"; from the Engine's perspective it is "which Adapter is active for this request". Switching Opponents during a Session is not supported in v1 — selection is made at Session start and persists until a new Session.
src/web/CONTEXT.md:28:- A **Session** is constituted by one **Opponent** (an Adapter), one human color choice, and a growing `move_history` (the same UCI list the Engine receives on every `/move` request). When a **Terminal state** is reached, the Session ends.
src/web/CONTEXT.md:29:- **Web ↔ Engine**: see `src/engine/CONTEXT.md`. Web sends Position + `adapter_id` + `move_history` per turn; Engine returns one legal Move. Web owns Session storage; the Engine is stateless per request.

.scratch/merge-gate-canary/07-artefacts/run-1/codex-review.json:65:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_33","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,130p' .scratch/merge-gate-canary/02.md"","aggregated_output":"# Canary 02 — silent exception swallow + bare 500 in `/move`

> **DO NOT MERGE.** This PR is a merge-gate canary; the branch
> existed solely to observe how the gate responds to a deliberate
> defect injection. See claude-harness-work issue #09.

## Path under test

`comment-only` — Codex medium + validator uphold expected → gate
surfaces finding in sticky but does **not** block (severity below
critical/high threshold).

## Injected defect

- **File touched**: `src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py`
- **Line(s)**: 28-42 (the `/move` handler body)
- **What changed**: wrapped the existing two-stage `try/except`
  body in an outer `try / except HTTPException: raise / except
  Exception: raise HTTPException(500) from None`. `HTTPException`
  is explicitly re-raised so the deliberate 422 paths still work
  and the pytest suite still passes; the defect is the new
  unobservable swallow of unexpected error classes.
- **Why it's a defect**: hides any unexpected exception type
  (programming error, upstream library bug, future regression)
  behind an opaque 500 with no traceback chaining and no log.
  Future incident triage cannot tell whether the 500 is a known
  422-like terminal state or a real bug.

## Expected outcome

- **Codex severity**: `medium` (observability gap; not a correctness
  bug because the handler still returns *some* error response)
- **Codex finding count (high+critical)**: 0
- **Validator verdict per finding**: `uphold` (no project context
  argues for swallowing exceptions silently)
- **Gate conclusion**: pass — sticky comment shows finding,
  blocking-count is 0, check stays green even under hard mode

## Run record

| Run | Commit | GH Actions run ID | Codex severity | Validator verdict | Sticky verdict | Notes |
|-----|--------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1   | 9b9d68f | [26391570063](https://github.com/lim010111/playlike-chess/actions/runs/26391570063) | 0/**1**/0/0 | HIGH uphold (1) | needs-attention, 1 blocking | severity grading mismatch (see below) |

Artefacts saved at `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/02-artefacts/run-1/`.

## Actual vs expected

✗ **Codex severity** — expected `medium`, got `high`. All other
fields followed the high grading consistently (1 high → 1 blocking
validator verdict → sticky shows 1 blocking under hard-mode
projection).

**Codex finding (verbatim):**

> [high] Catch-all handler suppresses unexpected endpoint failures
> — `src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py:42`. The new outer
> `except Exception` converts every unexpected runtime/programming
> error in `/move` into a bare `HTTPException(500)` with `from
> None`. That suppresses the original traceback/chaining and treats
> real bugs in FEN parsing internals, move selection, or response
> construction as ordinary handled HTTP errors. In practice this
> removes the diagnostic signal FastAPI/Starlette would normally
> surface for unhandled exceptions, making future engine or
> adapter failures much harder to detect and triage.

**Hypothesis.** Codex's adversarial-review prompt treats
observability gaps that hide diagnostic signal as `high` rather
than `medium`, because they directly impair incident response.
Producing a defect Codex *consistently* grades as `medium`
requires something more cosmetic (stale docstring, vacuous test,
unused import) — but those land in a different review register
and may not even be flagged at all.

**Comment-only path coverage.** Under the strict reading of #09
AC #3 ("all four paths covered — one canary per path"), this
canary does not exercise the comment-only path; it lands as a
second instance of the block path together with C1. The path is,
however, informally covered by **Phase 1 PR #10** itself: that
PR produced 2 medium findings + 0 critical/high, validator
upheld both at medium, sticky shown, check passed. That is
exactly the gate behaviour the comment-only path is meant to
exercise — observed on a real-feature PR rather than a
synthetic canary. We accept this informal proxy here rather than
re-injecting a less-severe defect for a third gate run; the
suite's value is the integrated gate observation, not the
specific PR-to-path bijection.

## Closing

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

from playlike_engine.api import app

STARTING_FEN = chess.STARTING_FEN
MID_GAME_FEN = "r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/2N2N2/PPPP1PPP/R1BQKB1R w KQkq - 4 4"
CHECKMATE_FEN = "rnb1kbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/6Pq/5P2/PPPPP2P/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 1 3"
INVALID_FEN_STR = "not a fen"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client() -> TestClient:
    return TestClient(app)


def test_starting_position_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": STARTING_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = response.json()
    assert set(body.keys()) == {"move_uci"}
    board = chess.Board(STARTING_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(body["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_mid_game_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": MID_GAME_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    board = chess.Board(MID_GAME_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(response.json()["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_invalid_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": INVALID_FEN_STR})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_checkmate_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": CHECKMATE_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_extra_field_rejected_by_strict_schema(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post(
        "/move",
        json={"fen": STARTING_FEN, "adapter_id": "magnus"},
    )
    assert response.status_code == 422

./docs/adr/0003-closed-roster-devtime-adapters.md:5:An open-roster extension is a natural v2 path — Adapter weights can be added to the deployment by retraining offline and shipping a new release without touching the serving code. The v1 scope deliberately excludes it.
./docs/adr/0002-split-data-sources.md:13:This is the reproducibility contract: any Adapter checkpoint released with v1 can be re-evaluated against its frozen snapshot at any later time and yield the same metrics. The "most recent ~10% hold-out" temporal split is computed against the snapshot, not the live archive — so the split is stable across re-evaluation regardless of how much new gameplay the Player has accumulated upstream.
./docs/adr/0004-project-name-playlike-chess.md:3:The project is named **Playlike Chess** (slug `playlike-chess`). The name lives in the repo identifier, the PRD header, this ADR set, and the README — never in user-facing UI copy, which exposes only Opponent names ("Playing against: Magnus Carlsen") per PRD User Story #8.
./docs/adr/0004-project-name-playlike-chess.md:5:The earlier working name "Imitation Engine" — surfaced in `.scratch/imitation-engine/PRD.md` v1 header and the seven tracer-bullet issue files — is retired. The rename collapses a glossary collision: `src/engine/CONTEXT.md` defines **Inference Engine** as the runtime module that wraps the Base model and all Roster Adapters, and `CONTEXT-MAP.md` already labels one of the three contexts simply "Engine". A project named "Imitation Engine" would force every future reference to disambiguate "project-level Engine" vs "module-level Engine" — exactly the kind of two-meanings-one-word problem the per-context glossaries' `_Avoid_` patterns are designed to prevent.
./docs/adr/0004-project-name-playlike-chess.md:7:The name **Playlike Chess** foregrounds the project's thesis as stated in the PRD Problem Statement: a chess engine "that lets the user pick a Player from a closed roster and play a complete game against an Adapter trained to imitate that Player's blitz move distribution". "Playlike" is the one-word form of "play like Magnus" / "play like Hikaru". The `-chess` suffix in the slug keeps the GitHub URL self-explanatory to portfolio-link skimmers without dragging the name into the UI.
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:21:2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's "same Position" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:32:Not domain language, but load-bearing v1 decisions:
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:34:- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:37:- Throughput ceiling ≈ 7–10 moves/sec on the 3080. Practical concurrent active-user limit ≈ 25 before PRD User Story #5's "couple of seconds" budget starts breaking; beyond ~35 the request queue grows unboundedly. v1 has no rate limiting — this cliff is accepted as the scale of a portfolio demo. Batched inference, multi-GPU, and explicit backpressure are deliberate v2 levers (see PRD Out of Scope).
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:42:- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked "is the Session over?", only "given this Position and history, what is your Move?". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:1:# Transformer over FEN tokens with policy head over fixed action space
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:3:We chose a Transformer that takes a single FEN string as tokenized text input and outputs logits over a fixed action space (~1968 UCI move encodings), rather than an LLM-style decoder over move-sequence tokens (α) or an AlphaZero-style ResNet over an 8×8×channels board tensor (β). This keeps the board state explicit (avoiding the implicit-board, long-context problem of (α)), makes illegal-move masking a one-line operation at the policy head, and aligns with LLM tooling for LoRA-based per-player adapters — strengths the other two options each lack.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:7:Per-Player Adapter strength relative to Base is treated as **empirical, not assumed**. Two competing forces are at play: (a) Adapter training data is materially higher-quality than Base data (Super-GM blitz vs ~1800-rated blitz), which could lift Adapter strength above Base; (b) v1's LoRA scope is rank-16 on attention QKVO only — FFN, embedding, and policy head are frozen — which constrains the Adapter to attention-routing reweights and prevents it from absorbing genuinely new chess knowledge that the 9M Base does not already represent. Either direction is plausible at v1: Adapter may modestly exceed, match, or trail Base.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:9:The strength comparison is measured by **direct head-to-head matches between Base and Base+Adapter** (100 games, blitz TC, alternating colors, sample decoding) — not by reading off external-pool ratings, since Elo is fundamentally a relative measure and the ~300-Elo-bucket Fairy-Stockfish ladder cannot resolve a 100-Elo gap. Adapter score percentage is converted to an Elo delta via the standard formula. >100 Elo regression (Adapter score < 36%) is the fail signal that triggers v2 LoRA-scope expansion (see PRD Out of Scope); anything within ±100 Elo of Base is treated as success on the strength axis, with stylistic divergence carried by opening repertoire KL.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:17:Known costs accepted for v1: path-conditioned style is not learnable — gambit follow-through, transposition-aware play, plan continuity from earlier in the Game. Threefold-repetition awareness must be added at the server's legality layer rather than learned by the model. AlphaZero feeds T=8 prior board-state planes precisely for these reasons; we are deliberately not following that pattern.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:19:Revisit trigger: if v1 evaluation or qualitative review shows clear path-conditioning deficits (transposition-zone errors, abandoned gambit themes), introduce a small history window (e.g. T=4 prior board states) and shrink the model to fit. Until then, path-dependence is out of scope.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:27:**Encoder-only with a single readout vector → linear projection** is locked even though the rest is deferred: it bounds the forward graph (no causal mask over the FEN tokens, no autoregressive decoding loop) so downstream modules (Inference Engine, legality mask application, decoding modes) can be specified concretely now without waiting on sub-architecture decisions.

## 3. Canary-PR guide

Canary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway
branches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known
risk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a
known answer key.

### What to canary

At minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt
prioritises:

| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |
| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |
| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |
| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |

`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*
under hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but
does not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always
non-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.

### Operating canaries

- Each canary's brief lives at
  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,
  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.
- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.
- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the
  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).
- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.

### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary

A "Codex false positive" on a canary means Codex flagged something
the brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:

1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem

.scratch/merge-gate-canary/03-artefacts/run-1/validators.json:5:      "raw_stdout": "The code at line 41–42 confirms exactly what Codex describes: `token = _new_engine_session_token()` is generated and `log.info(\"engine session token: %s\", token)` logs it server-side, but `MoveResponse` (line 34–36) contains only `move_uci` and the token is never returned to the client or set as a response header.\n\nHowever, the function's docstring on line 24 says `\"Per-request session token logged for client-side request correlation.\"` — the phrase \"logged for client-side request correlation\" is the stated purpose Codex challenges. The git commit message for this change explicitly labels this a canary test (`[canary] C3 insecure RNG for session token`) and describes the feature as a deliberate construct to test the validator, noting the expected finding from Codex is insecure RNG — not the correlation-exposure gap this finding targets.\n\nThe commit message does not document any design decision that the token is intentionally server-side-only (and the docstring's \"client-side request correlation\" language directly contradicts that idea). No ADR, CONTEXT.md, or doc passage sanctions a design where a \"client-side correlation\" token is never sent to the client. The finding holds up against the code as written.\n\n[LOW] uphold src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py:41 — src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py:42: log.info(\"engine session token: %s\", token) — token never included in MoveResponse or response headers\n\nblock_count: 0\nbypass_eligible: yes\naction: merge ok\n",
.scratch/merge-gate-canary/03-artefacts/run-1/codex-review.json:18:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_8","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate-operations.md\"","aggregated_output":"<!-- generated by /setup-merge-gate v1; do not edit by hand -->\n\n# Merge gate — operations playbook\n\nThis document is the human-facing playbook for the **Merge gate** —\nthe **Gate** for the pre-merge phase of the **Harness** (see\n`CONTEXT-MAP.md`). The gate runs Codex's adversarial review as the\nprimary reviewer and a Claude validator (MVP) that classifies each\nfinding against project context before the gate decides to block.\nThe MVP runs a single Claude validator per\n[ADR-0005](adr/0005-claude-only-validator-mvp-gemini-deferred.md);\na second validator returns once issue #10's soft-mode measurement\ndata is in.\n\nThe **Setup skill** (`/setup-merge-gate`) installs the gate's\nartifacts; it is not itself a gate. This playbook describes how to\n*operate* the installed gate.\n\nGlossary terms (`Harness`, `Gate`, `Audited bypass lane`,\n`Oracle weakening`) are defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md` — do not redefine\nthem here; link or quote.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Operational phases at a glance\n\n| Phase | Branch protection | Gate behaviour | Typical use |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Soft** | Not required | Runs, posts findings, **never blocks** | Data-collection — every PR is a measurement |\n| **Hard** | Required check | Blocks merge on `critical` or `high` ∩ `uphold` | Steady state — gate is trusted |\n\nMode is recorded in `harness.toml` under `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default`\n(string boolean: `\"true\"` = soft, `\"false\"` = hard). The flip from\n`\"true\"` to `\"false\"` is HITL and is governed by §2 below.\n\nThe gate's check name is stable — `merge-gate / codex-review` — so\nbranch-protection references do not need updating across mode flips.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Soft → Hard promotion checklist\n\nThe flip from `soft` to `hard` is the single moment the gate's\nauthority changes. Treat it as a release, not a tweak. All items\nmust be checked, and the human sign-off line must be filled in.\n\n### Quantitative gates\n\n- [ ] **N ≥ 10 non-canary PRs** measured under soft mode (canary\n      PRs are excluded — they are deliberate defect injections, not\n      organic traffic).\n- [ ] **False-positive rate ≤ 1** across the N=10 set, where a false\n      positive is *validator said block, human said \"I would not have\n      wanted this blocked\"*.\n- [ ] **False-negative rate recorded** — *validator said pass, human\n      said \"I would have wanted this blocked\"*. A high FNR is **not**\n      a blocker for the soft→hard flip (the gate's purpose is to\n      cut false negatives over time), but it must be written down so\n      the next iteration can target it.\n\n### Data layout\n\nEach PR's soft-mode data lives in the project's plan repo under\n`.scratch/merge-gate/runs/<pr-number>.md` and records:\n\n- Codex JSON output (artefact link or inline)\n- Claude verdict\n- Final decision the gate would have made\n- Human verdict: would I have wanted this blocked? (yes / no / unsure)\n- One-line rationale if validator and human diverged\n\nA second validator's verdict slot will be added to this layout when\nissue #10's measurement motivates re-introducing it.\n\n### Qualitative review\n\n- [ ] Skim every entry. Look for *patterns* in the false positives\n      (e.g. \"Codex keeps flagging our intentional retry-without-cap\n      because AGENTS.md does not state the upstream timeout\n      contract\") rather than treating each as isolated noise. A\n      pattern is a signal to tighten the validator's project\n      references (§5), not to lower the bar.\n- [ ] Confirm the **Audited bypass lane** was never silently used in\n      this window — every `merge-gate-bypass` label application\n      should appear in §4's audit log.\n\n### Human sign-off\n\n> Signed off by: `<name>`\n> Date: `<YYYY-MM-DD>`\n> N = `<count>` PRs · FPR = `<count>/<N>`\n> Flipping `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default` from `\"true\"` to `\"false\"` and enabling the\n> branch-protection rule on `merge-gate / codex-review`.\n\n### After the flip\n\n- Update `harness.toml`: `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \"false\"`.\n- Enable the branch-protection rule (the `/setup-merge-gate` install\n  output printed the exact rule text — keep it in the install log).\n- Announce the flip in whatever channel the team uses; the first\n  organic block under hard mode is the moment people learn the gate\n  is real.\n\nIf any quantitative gate is unmet: **do not flip**. Open or update\nthe soft→hard tracking issue with what is missing and which upstream\nissue is the fix path (tighten validator prompt, expand AGENTS.md\ncontext, etc.).\n\n---\n\n## 3. Canary-PR guide\n\nCanary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway\nbranches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known\nrisk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a\nknown answer key.\n\n### What to canary\n\nAt minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt\nprioritises:\n\n| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |\n| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\n| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\n| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |\n\n`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*\nunder hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but\ndoes not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always\nnon-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.\n\n### Operating canaries\n\n- Each canary's brief lives at\n  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,\n  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.\n- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.\n- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the\n  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).\n- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.\n\n### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary\n\nA \"Codex false positive\" on a canary means Codex flagged something\nthe brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:\n\n1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem\n   unrelated to the injection. Record it as a found bug in the\n   canary brief — that is a useful side effect, not noise.\n2. **Context starvation.** Codex flagged code that looks risky in\n   isolation but is safe given project conventions that AGENTS.md\n   does not yet spell out. This is the validator's job to dismiss\n   with a citation (γ Strict — see §5). If the validator also fails\n   to dismiss, treat it as a *project-context gap*: open an issue per\n   §4 tagged `merge-gate-fp` with a pointer to the AGENTS.md /\n   `CONTEXT-MAP.md` text that should have made the dismissal possible.\n\n### Interpreting a missed canary\n\nIf a canary's expected severity is `critical` but Codex returns\n`medium`, or returns nothing, record it in the brief with a short\nhypothesis (e.g. \"Codex missed the rollback case — likely because\nthe diff did not include the migration file\"). Missed canaries are\nthe input for tightening the canary suite and/or the Codex prompt\nin a later iteration — they are not blockers for soft→hard.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Audited bypass lane — the `merge-gate-bypass` label\n\nThe merge gate provides an **Audited bypass lane** in the sense\ndefined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md`: an acknowledged, recorded way to pass the\ngate without satisfying it. The lane exists so emergencies are not\nforced through the silent-circumvention door. It is **not** a\nshortcut for routine merges.\n\n### When the label is allowed\n\n- **Production incident hotfix** where the cost of waiting for the\n  gate exceeds the cost of a missed finding, and the fix is small\n  enough for a human to review end-to-end.\n- **Confirmed Codex false positive** that the validator could not\n  dismiss with a citation, where the team has already opened a\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue per §5 and is unwilling to block the PR on\n  the fix.\n- **Infrastructure / docs-only PR** that should have hit the\n  `docs-only` path glob but did not (treat the missed glob as a\n  bug — fix the workflow's glob in a follow-up).\n\nThe label is **never** appropriate for:\n\n- \"The gate is slow today\" — that is a §7 cost/latency issue, file\n  it there.\n- \"I disagree with the finding\" without a written argument — open a\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue and resolve through §5, not the bypass lane.\n- Avoiding the validator `unsure` runbook in §6 — bypass is the\n  lane of last resort, not the default for `unsure`.\n\n### Approval\n\n- **Single approver model.** One human who is not the PR author must\n  apply the `merge-gate-bypass` label and leave a PR comment with\n  the reason (one of the three categories above) and a link to any\n  follow-up issue.\n- For production-incident hotfixes, the approver is the on-call;\n  for the other two categories, the approver is a project\n  maintainer.\n\n### Audit trail\n\n- The label application is itself a GitHub event; that is the\n  primary audit record.\n- The approver's PR comment is the human-readable rationale.\n- A follow-up entry is added to\n  `.scratch/merge-gate/bypass-log.md` in the plan repo within one\n  business day: PR link, approver, category, follow-up issue link.\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

-lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"<!-- generated by /setup-merge-gate v1; do not edit by hand -->\\n# Merge gate — quick reference\\n\\nThis project runs the **merge-gate** CI workflow on every pull\\nrequest. The gate is the pre-merge phase of the harness; the full\\noperations playbook (canary suite, soft→hard promotion, validator\\ndisagreement runbook, roll-back) lives at\\n[`docs/merge-gate-operations.md`](merge-gate-operations.md).\\n\\n## Blocking rule\\n\\n`critical` or `high` severity Codex findings classified `uphold` by\\nthe Claude validator block merge. `medium` / `low` findings are\\ncommented but never block. See ADR-0005 for why this MVP runs the\\nClaude validator only (Gemini concordance is deferred pending\\nsoft-mode measurement data).\\n\\n## Severity matrix\\n\\n| Severity | Validator `uphold` | Validator `dismiss` (cited) | Validator `unsure` |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| critical | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\\n| high     | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\\n| medium   | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\\n| low      | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\\n\\n## Bypass label\\n\\nApply the `merge-gate-bypass` label to a PR to skip the gate. Bypass\\nis the **audited bypass lane** — one human (not the PR author) must\\napply the label, leave a PR comment with the reason, and add an\\nentry to the plan repo's bypass log within one business day. See\\noperations playbook §4 for the full bypass policy.\\n\\n## Soft → Hard promotion\\n\\nThe gate ships in **soft mode** (`harness.toml`\\n`[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \\\"true\\\"`): runs, reports, never\\nblocks. Flip to hard mode after meeting the quantitative gates in\\noperations playbook §2 (N ≥ 10 PRs, FPR ≤ 1, human sign-off) **and**\\nenabling the branch-protection rule on the check\\n`merge-gate / codex-review`.\\n\\n## Local re-installation\\n\\nThe skill `/setup-merge-gate` is idempotent. Re-running it shows a\\ndiff for the `[merge-gate]` section of `harness.toml` and prompts\\nonce; vendored agent and skill files under `.claude/` are overwritten\\nunconditionally. `--uninstall` removes the workflow, the\\n`[merge-gate]` section, the vendored copies, and this document (only\\nif its first-line marker is intact).\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/CONTEXT.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/CONTEXT.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Engine\\n\\nThe runtime that, given a Position and a Player choice, returns a Move. Loads the Base model and all Roster Adapters at startup and selects an Adapter per request.\\n\\n## Language\\n\\n**Inference Engine**:\\nThe runtime module wrapping the Base model and all Roster Adapters. Exposes `(Position, adapter_id, decoding_mode) → Move (+ optional policy distribution)`. Handles legality masking, decoding-mode dispatch, and per-request Adapter selection. Distinct from the Web API layer that calls it.\\n_Avoid_: bare \\\"model\\\" (overloaded — see Training CONTEXT for **Base model** vs **Adapter**)\\n\\n**Decoding mode**:\\nHow a Move is chosen from the policy logits over legal Moves. Two modes:\\n- **Greedy** — deterministic argmax over the masked logits. Used by the Evaluation Harness for the top-1 Move-match metric.\\n- **Sample** — temperature-scaled softmax sampling (default temperature 0.7). Used in live gameplay so repeating an opening does not produce identical Games.\\n\\nOrthogonal to Player choice (any Adapter can be played in either mode) and to legality (mask applies in both).\\n\\n**Legality mask**:\\nThe boolean tensor over the 1968-action UCI space applied before decoding to set illegal-Move logits to -∞. Computed in two stages:\\n1. **Rule legality** — `python-chess` `board.legal_moves` from the FEN. Covers all chess rules whose verdict depends only on the current Position (piece movement, check, pin, en-passant, castling rights encoded in FEN).\\n2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's \\\"same Position\\\" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).\\n\\nThe 50-move-rule clock lives in the FEN and is already handled by stage 1. 5-fold and 75-move (automatic) are detected client-side as game-over events; the Inference Engine itself does not refuse to move once they occur.\\n\\n_Avoid_: \\\"legal moves filter\\\" — **mask** is the canonical term.\\n\\n**Policy distribution**:\\nThe post-mask, post-softmax distribution over the 1968 UCI actions for a given Position+Adapter+decoding-mode. Returned optionally for the comparison panel and the Evaluation Harness.\\n\\n## Implementation notes\\n\\nNot domain language, but load-bearing v1 decisions:\\n\\n- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.\\n- Adapter selection is **in-place per request**: the worker activates the requested Adapter, runs the forward pass, returns the Move, then accepts the next request. Two simultaneous requests for different Adapters are safe because they cannot overlap on the GPU (the lock guarantees no interleaved state mutation).\\n- The server is **stateless per request**. Game state lives in the browser (`chess.js`). The same Position with the same Adapter under greedy decoding returns the same Move every time; under sampling, varied.\\n- Throughput ceiling ≈ 7–10 moves/sec on the 3080. Practical concurrent active-user limit ≈ 25 before PRD User Story #5's \\\"couple of seconds\\\" budget starts breaking; beyond ~35 the request queue grows unboundedly. v1 has no rate limiting — this cliff is accepted as the scale of a portfolio demo. Batched inference, multi-GPU, and explicit backpressure are deliberate v2 levers (see PRD Out of Scope).\\n\\n## Relationships\\n\\n- **Training → Engine**: The Inference Engine consumes two artifact families produced by Training — one Base model checkpoint and one Adapter file per Roster Player. Engine never re-trains or modifies these artifacts.\\n- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked \\\"is the Session over?\\\", only \\\"given this Position and history, what is your Move?\\\".
