The gate runs Codex's adversarial review as the\\nprimary reviewer and a Claude validator (MVP) that classifies each\\nfinding against project context before the gate decides to block.\\nThe MVP runs a single Claude validator per\\n[ADR-0005](adr/0005-claude-only-validator-mvp-gemini-deferred.md);\\na second validator returns once issue #10's soft-mode measurement\\ndata is in.\\n\\nThe **Setup skill** (`/setup-merge-gate`) installs the gate's\\nartifacts; it is not itself a gate. This playbook describes how to\\n*operate* the installed gate.\\n\\nGlossary terms (`Harness`, `Gate`, `Audited bypass lane`,\\n`Oracle weakening`) are defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md` — do not redefine\\nthem here; link or quote.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. Operational phases at a glance\\n\\n| Phase | Branch protection | Gate behaviour | Typical use |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| **Soft** | Not required | Runs, posts findings, **never blocks** | Data-collection — every PR is a measurement |\\n| **Hard** | Required check | Blocks merge on `critical` or `high` ∩ `uphold` | Steady state — gate is trusted |\\n\\nMode is recorded in `harness.toml` under `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default`\\n(string boolean: `\\\"true\\\"` = soft, `\\\"false\\\"` = hard). The flip from\\n`\\\"true\\\"` to `\\\"false\\\"` is HITL and is governed by §2 below.\\n\\nThe gate's check name is stable — `merge-gate / codex-review` — so\\nbranch-protection references do not need updating across mode flips.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Soft → Hard promotion checklist\\n\\nThe flip from `soft` to `hard` is the single moment the gate's\\nauthority changes. Treat it as a release, not a tweak. All items\\nmust be checked, and the human sign-off line must be filled in.\\n\\n### Quantitative gates\\n\\n- [ ] **N ≥ 10 non-canary PRs** measured under soft mode (canary\\n      PRs are excluded — they are deliberate defect injections, not\\n      organic traffic).\\n- [ ] **False-positive rate ≤ 1** across the N=10 set, where a false\\n      positive is *validator said block, human said \\\"I would not have\\n      wanted this blocked\\\"*.\\n- [ ] **False-negative rate recorded** — *validator said pass, human\\n      said \\\"I would have wanted this blocked\\\"*. A high FNR is **not**\\n      a blocker for the soft→hard flip (the gate's purpose is to\\n      cut false negatives over time), but it must be written down so\\n      the next iteration can target it.\\n\\n### Data layout\\n\\nEach PR's soft-mode data lives in the project's plan repo under\\n`.scratch/merge-gate/runs/<pr-number>.md` and records:\\n\\n- Codex JSON output (artefact link or inline)\\n- Claude verdict\\n- Final decision the gate would have made\\n- Human verdict: would I have wanted this blocked? (yes / no / unsure)\\n- One-line rationale if validator and human diverged\\n\\nA second validator's verdict slot will be added to this layout when\\nissue #10's measurement motivates re-introducing it.\\n\\n### Qualitative review\\n\\n- [ ] Skim every entry. Look for *patterns* in the false positives\\n      (e.g. \\\"Codex keeps flagging our intentional retry-without-cap\\n      because AGENTS.md does not state the upstream timeout\\n      contract\\\") rather than treating each as isolated noise. A\\n      pattern is a signal to tighten the validator's project\\n      references (§5), not to lower the bar.\\n- [ ] Confirm the **Audited bypass lane** was never silently used in\\n      this window — every `merge-gate-bypass` label application\\n      should appear in §4's audit log.\\n\\n### Human sign-off\\n\\n> Signed off by: `<name>`\\n> Date: `<YYYY-MM-DD>`\\n> N = `<count>` PRs · FPR = `<count>/<N>`\\n> Flipping `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default` from `\\\"true\\\"` to `\\\"false\\\"` and enabling the\\n> branch-protection rule on `merge-gate / codex-review`.\\n\\n### After the flip\\n\\n- Update `harness.toml`: `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \\\"false\\\"`.\\n- Enable the branch-protection rule (the `/setup-merge-gate` install\\n  output printed the exact rule text — keep it in the install log).\\n- Announce the flip in whatever channel the team uses; the first\\n  organic block under hard mode is the moment people learn the gate\\n  is real.\\n\\nIf any quantitative gate is unmet: **do not flip**. Open or update\\nthe soft→hard tracking issue with what is missing and which upstream\\nissue is the fix path (tighten validator prompt, expand AGENTS.md\\ncontext, etc.).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. Canary-PR guide\\n\\nCanary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway\\nbranches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known\\nrisk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a\\nknown answer key.\\n\\n### What to canary\\n\\nAt minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt\\nprioritises:\\n\\n| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |\\n|---|---|---|---|\\n| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\\n| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |\\n\\n`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*\\nunder hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but\\ndoes not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always\\nnon-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.\\n\\n### Operating canaries\\n\\n- Each canary's brief lives at\\n  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,\\n  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.\\n- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.\\n- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the\\n  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).\\n- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.\\n\\n### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary\\n\\nA \\\"Codex false positive\\\" on a canary means Codex flagged something\\nthe brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:\\n\\n1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem\\n   unrelated to the injection. Record it as a found bug in the\\n   canary brief — that is a useful side effect, not noise.\\n2. **Context starvation.** Codex flagged code that looks risky in\\n   isolation but is safe given project conventions that AGENTS.md\\n   does not yet spell out. This is the validator's job to dismiss\\n   with a citation (γ Strict — see §5). If the validator also fails\\n   to dismiss, treat it as a *project-context gap*: open an issue per\\n   §4 tagged `merge-gate-fp` with a pointer to the AGENTS.md /\\n   `CONTEXT-MAP.md` text that should have made the dismissal possible.\\n\\n### Interpreting a missed canary\\n\\nIf a canary's expected severity is `critical` but Codex returns\\n`medium`, or returns nothing, record it in the brief with a short\\nhypothesis (e.g. \\\"Codex missed the rollback case — likely because\\nthe diff did not include the migration file\\\"). Missed canaries are\\nthe input for tightening the canary suite and/or the Codex prompt\\nin a later iteration — they are not blockers for soft→hard.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Audited bypass lane — the `merge-gate-bypass` label\\n\\nThe merge gate provides an **Audited bypass lane** in the sense\\ndefined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md`: an acknowledged, recorded way to pass the\\ngate without satisfying it. The lane exists so emergencies are not\\nforced through the silent-circumvention door. It is **not** a\\nshortcut for routine merges.\\n\\n### When the label is allowed\\n\\n- **Production incident hotfix** where the cost of waiting for the\\n  gate exceeds the cost of a missed finding, and the fix is small\\n  enough for a human to review end-to-end.\\n- **Confirmed Codex false positive** that the validator could not\\n  dismiss with a citation, where the team has already opened a\\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue per §5 and is unwilling to block the PR on\\n  the fix.\\n- **Infrastructure / docs-only PR** that should have hit the\\n  `docs-only` path glob but did not (treat the missed glob as a\\n  bug — fix the workflow's glob in a follow-up).\\n\\nThe label is **never** appropriate for:\\n\\n- \\\"The gate is slow today\\\" — that is a §7 cost/latency issue, file\\n  it there.\\n- \\\"I disagree with the finding\\\" without a written argument — open a\\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue and resolve through §5, not the bypass lane.\\n- Avoiding the validator `unsure` runbook in §6 — bypass is the\\n  lane of last resort, not the default for `unsure`.\\n\\n### Approval\\n\\n- **Single approver model.** One human who is not the PR author must\\n  apply the `merge-gate-bypass` label and leave a PR comment with\\n  the reason (one of the three categories above) and a link to any\\n  follow-up issue.\\n- For production-incident hotfixes, the approver is the on-call;\\n  for the other two categories, the approver is a project\\n  maintainer.\\n\\n### Audit trail\\n\\n- The label application is itself a GitHub event; that is the\\n  primary audit record.\\n- The approver's PR comment is the human-readable rationale.\\n- A follow-up entry is added to\\n  `.scratch/merge-gate/bypass-log.md` in the plan repo within one\\n  business day: PR link, approver, category, follow-up issue link.\\n\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"nl -ba src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py | sed -n '1,140p'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_9\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/bash -lc \\\"nl -ba src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py | sed -n '1,140p'\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"     1\\t\\\"\\\"\\\"FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.\\n     2\\t\\n     3\\tPhase 1 (random-move tracer): exposes POST /move that returns a uniformly\\n     4\\trandom legal move for the supplied FEN. The same Position with the same Adapter under greedy decoding returns the same Move every time; under sampling, varied.\\n./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:37:- Throughput ceiling ≈ 7–10 moves/sec on the 3080. Practical concurrent active-user limit ≈ 25 before PRD User Story #5's \\\"couple of seconds\\\" budget starts breaking; beyond ~35 the request queue grows unboundedly. v1 has no rate limiting — this cliff is accepted as the scale of a portfolio demo. Batched inference, multi-GPU, and explicit backpressure are deliberate v2 levers (see PRD Out of Scope).\\n./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:42:- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked \\\"is the Session over?\\\", only \\\"given this Position and history, what is your Move?\\\". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.\\n./docs/adr/0003-closed-roster-devtime-adapters.md:3:Per-Player Adapters are trained at dev-time for a fixed Roster of named Players and shipped with each release; the runtime does not accept arbitrary Player names from end-users to train new Adapters on demand. The decision rejects the open-roster alternative because runtime training would require a job queue, GPU scheduling, failure handling, and abuse mitigation — infrastructure that would expand scope significantly past the (C)-grade usable-tool target and would also produce Adapter quality that varies per request (no human in the loop to validate hyperparameters or evaluation results before exposing the Adapter to users).\\n./docs/merge-gate-operations.md:326:| Codex token cost per gate run | TBD (#10 measurement) | same as soft | Adversarial-review prompt + diff payload |\\n./docs/merge-gate-operations.md:327:| Validator token cost per gate run | TBD (#10 measurement) | same as soft | One Claude validator pass over the Codex JSON (single-model MVP per ADR-0005) |\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:1:# Transformer over FEN tokens with policy head over fixed action space\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:3:We chose a Transformer that takes a single FEN string as tokenized text input and outputs logits over a fixed action space (~1968 UCI move encodings), rather than an LLM-style decoder over move-sequence tokens (α) or an AlphaZero-style ResNet over an 8×8×channels board tensor (β). This keeps the board state explicit (avoiding the implicit-board, long-context problem of (α)), makes illegal-move masking a one-line operation at the policy head, and aligns with LLM tooling for LoRA-based per-player adapters — strengths the other two options each lack.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:15:The input is a single FEN per request: no move history, no prior board states. Accepted because (a) at 9M params / 10GB VRAM the model cannot meaningfully learn long-horizon plans even if history were available, (b) adding history would multiply sequence length ~6–8× and force the model to shrink, and (c) per-Player archives are small (~10K Games), so same-FEN generalization across Games matters more than path-conditioning.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.\\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:27:**Encoder-only with a single readout vector → linear projection** is locked even though the rest is deferred: it bounds the forward graph (no causal mask over the FEN tokens, no autoregressive decoding loop) so downstream modules (Inference Engine, legality mask application, decoding modes) can be specified concretely now without waiting on sub-architecture .scratch/merge-gate-canary/05-artefacts/run-1/codex-review.json:17:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_7","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,260p' src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py"","aggregated_output":""""FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.

Phase 1 (random-move tracer): exposes POST /move that returns a uniformly
random legal move for the supplied FEN. No ML, no adapters.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

from playlike_engine.random_move import NoLegalMovesError, pick_random_legal_move

app = FastAPI(title="Playlike Chess Engine", version="0.0.1")


def verify_auth_token(token: str) -> bool:
    """Verify a client-supplied auth token."""
    return True


class MoveRequest(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    fen: str


class MoveResponse(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    move_uci: str


@app.post("/move", response_model=MoveResponse)
def move(req: MoveRequest, authorization: str = Header(default="")) -> MoveResponse:
    verify_auth_token(authorization)
    try:
        board = chess.Board(req.fen)
    except ValueError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=f"invalid FEN: {exc}") from exc
    try:
        chosen = pick_random_legal_move(board)
    except NoLegalMovesError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=str(exc)) from exc
    return MoveResponse(move_uci=chosen.uci())
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

from playlike_engine.api import app

STARTING_FEN = chess.STARTING_FEN
MID_GAME_FEN = "r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/2N2N2/PPPP1PPP/R1BQKB1R w KQkq - 4 4"
CHECKMATE_FEN = "rnb1kbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/6Pq/5P2/PPPPP2P/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 1 3"
INVALID_FEN_STR = "not a fen"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client() -> TestClient:
    return TestClient(app)


def test_starting_position_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": STARTING_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = response.json()
    assert set(body.keys()) == {"move_uci"}
    board = chess.Board(STARTING_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(body["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_mid_game_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": MID_GAME_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    board = chess.Board(MID_GAME_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(response.json()["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_invalid_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": INVALID_FEN_STR})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_checkmate_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": CHECKMATE_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_extra_field_rejected_by_strict_schema(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post(
        "/move",
        json={"fen": STARTING_FEN, "adapter_id": "magnus"},
    )
    assert response.status_code == 422

./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:1:# Transformer over FEN tokens with policy head over fixed action space
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:3:We chose a Transformer that takes a single FEN string as tokenized text input and outputs logits over a fixed action space (~1968 UCI move encodings), rather than an LLM-style decoder over move-sequence tokens (α) or an AlphaZero-style ResNet over an 8×8×channels board tensor (β). This keeps the board state explicit (avoiding the implicit-board, long-context problem of (α)), makes illegal-move masking a one-line operation at the policy head, and aligns with LLM tooling for LoRA-based per-player adapters — strengths the other two options each lack.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.
./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:27:**Encoder-only with a single readout vector → linear projection** is locked even though the rest is deferred: it bounds the forward graph (no causal mask over the FEN tokens, no autoregressive decoding loop) so downstream modules (Inference Engine, legality mask application, decoding modes) can be specified concretely now without waiting on sub-architecture decisions.

 """FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.
 
 Phase 1 (random-move tracer): exposes POST /move that returns a uniformly
 random legal move for the supplied FEN. No ML, no adapters.
 """
 
 from __future__ import annotations
 
+import logging
+import random
+import string
+
 import chess
 from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
 from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict
 
 from playlike_engine.random_move import NoLegalMovesError, pick_random_legal_move
 
 app = FastAPI(title="Playlike Chess Engine", version="0.0.1")
+log = logging.getLogger("playlike_engine.api")
+
+
+def _new_engine_session_token() -> str:
+    """Per-request session token logged for client-side request correlation."""
+    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits
+    return "".join(random.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(16))
 
 
 class MoveRequest(BaseModel):
     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
     fen: str
 
 
 class MoveResponse(BaseModel):
     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
     move_uci: str
 
 
 @app.post("/move", response_model=MoveResponse)
 def move(req: MoveRequest) -> MoveResponse:
+    token = _new_engine_session_token()
+    log.info("engine session token: %s", token)
     try:
         board = chess.Board(req.fen)
     except ValueError as exc:
         raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=f"invalid FEN: {exc}") from exc
     try:
         chosen = pick_random_legal_move(board)
     except NoLegalMovesError as exc:
         raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=str(exc)) from exc
     return MoveResponse(move_uci=chosen.uci())

# Merge gate — operations playbook

This document is the human-facing playbook for the **Merge gate** —
the **Gate** for the pre-merge phase of the **Harness** (see
`CONTEXT-MAP.md`). The gate runs Codex's adversarial review as the
primary reviewer and a Claude validator (MVP) that classifies each
finding against project context before the gate decides to block.
The MVP runs a single Claude validator per
[ADR-0005](adr/0005-claude-only-validator-mvp-gemini-deferred.md);
a second validator returns once issue #10's soft-mode measurement
data is in.

The **Setup skill** (`/setup-merge-gate`) installs the gate's
artifacts; it is not itself a gate. This playbook describes how to
*operate* the installed gate.

Glossary terms (`Harness`, `Gate`, `Audited bypass lane`,
`Oracle weakening`) are defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md` — do not redefine
them here; link or quote.

---

## 1. Operational phases at a glance

| Phase | Branch protection | Gate behaviour | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft** | Not required | Runs, posts findings, **never blocks** | Data-collection — every PR is a measurement |
| **Hard** | Required check | Blocks merge on `critical` or `high` ∩ `uphold` | Steady state — gate is trusted |

Mode is recorded in `harness.toml` under `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default`
(string boolean: `"true"` = soft, `"false"` = hard). The flip from
`"true"` to `"false"` is HITL and is governed by §2 below.

The gate's check name is stable — `merge-gate / codex-review` — so
branch-protection references do not need updating across mode flips.

---

## 2. Soft → Hard promotion checklist

The flip from `soft` to `hard` is the single moment the gate's
authority changes. Treat it as a release, not a tweak. All items
must be checked, and the human sign-off line must be filled in.

### Quantitative gates

- [ ] **N ≥ 10 non-canary PRs** measured under soft mode (canary
      PRs are excluded — they are deliberate defect injections, not
      organic traffic).
- [ ] **False-positive rate ≤ 1** across the N=10 set, where a false
      positive is *validator said block, human said "I would not have
      wanted this blocked"*.
- [ ] **False-negative rate recorded** — *validator said pass, human
      said "I would have wanted this blocked"*. A high FNR is **not**
      a blocker for the soft→hard flip (the gate's purpose is to
      cut false negatives over time), but it must be written down so
      the next iteration can target it.

### Data layout

Each PR's soft-mode data lives in the project's plan repo under
`.scratch/merge-gate/runs/<pr-number>.md` and records:

- Codex JSON output (artefact link or inline)
- Claude verdict
- Final decision the gate would have made
- Human verdict: would I have wanted this blocked? (yes / no / unsure)
- One-line rationale if validator and human diverged

A second validator's verdict slot will be added to this layout when
issue #10's measurement motivates re-introducing it.

### Qualitative review

- [ ] Skim every entry. Look for *patterns* in the false positives
      (e.g. "Codex keeps flagging our intentional retry-without-cap
      because AGENTS.md does not state the upstream timeout
      contract") rather than treating each as isolated noise. A
      pattern is a signal to tighten the validator's project
      references (§5), not to lower the bar.
- [ ] Confirm the **Audited bypass lane** was never silently used in
      this window — every `merge-gate-bypass` label application
      should appear in §4's audit log.

### Human sign-off

> Signed off by: `<name>`
> Date: `<YYYY-MM-DD>`
> N = `<count>` PRs · FPR = `<count>/<N>`
> Flipping `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default` from `"true"` to `"false"` and enabling the
> branch-protection rule on `merge-gate / codex-review`.

### After the flip

- Update `harness.toml`: `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = "false"`.
- Enable the branch-protection rule (the `/setup-merge-gate` install
  output printed the exact rule text — keep it in the install log).
- Announce the flip in whatever channel the team uses; the first
  organic block under hard mode is the moment people learn the gate
  is real.

If any quantitative gate is unmet: **do not flip**. Open or update
the soft→hard tracking issue with what is missing and which upstream
issue is the fix path (tighten validator prompt, expand AGENTS.md
context, etc.).

---

## 3. Canary-PR guide

Canary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway
branches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known
risk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a
known answer key.

### What to canary

At minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt
prioritises:

| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |
| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |
| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |
| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |

`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*
under hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but
does not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always
non-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.

### Operating canaries

- Each canary's brief lives at
  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,
  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.
- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.
- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the
  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).
- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.

### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary

A "Codex false positive" on a canary means Codex flagged something
the brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:

1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem
   unrelated to the injection. Record it as a found bug in the
   canary brief — that is a useful side effect, not noise.
2. **Context starvation.** Codex flagged code that looks risky in
   isolation but is safe given project conventions that AGENTS.md
   does not yet spell out. This is the validator's job to dismiss
   with a citation (γ Strict — see §5). If the validator also fails
   to dismiss, treat it as a *project-context gap*: open an issue per
   §4 tagged `merge-gate-fp` with a pointer to the AGENTS.md /
   `CONTEXT-MAP.md` text that should have made the dismissal possible.

### Interpreting a missed canary

If a canary's expected severity is `critical` but Codex returns
`medium`, or returns nothing, record it in the brief with a short
hypothesis (e.g. "Codex missed the rollback case — likely because
the diff did not include the migration file"). Missed canaries are
the input for tightening the canary suite and/or the Codex prompt
in a later iteration — they are not blockers for soft→hard.

---

## 4. Audited bypass lane — the `merge-gate-bypass` label

The merge gate provides an **Audited bypass lane** in the sense
defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md`: an acknowledged, recorded way to pass the
gate without satisfying it. The lane exists so emergencies are not
forced through the silent-circumvention door. It is **not** a
shortcut for routine merges.

### When the label is allowed

- **Production incident hotfix** where the cost of waiting for the
  gate exceeds the cost of a missed finding, and the fix is small
  enough for a human to review end-to-end.
- **Confirmed Codex false positive** that the validator could not
  dismiss with a citation, where the team has already opened a
  `merge-gate-fp` issue per §5 and is unwilling to block the PR on
  the fix.
- **Infrastructure / docs-only PR** that should have hit the
  `docs-only` path glob but did not (treat the missed glob as a
  bug — fix the workflow's glob in a follow-up).

The label is **never** appropriate for:

- "The gate is slow today" — that is a §7 cost/latency issue, file
  it there.
- "I disagree with the finding" without a written argument — open a
  `merge-gate-fp` issue and resolve through §5, not the bypass lane.
- Avoiding the validator `unsure` runbook in §6 — bypass is the
  lane of last resort, not the default for `unsure`.

### Approval

- **Single approver model.** One human who is not the PR author must
  apply the `merge-gate-bypass` label and leave a PR comment with
  the reason (one of the three categories above) and a link to any
  follow-up issue.
- For production-incident hotfixes, the approver is the on-call;
  for the other two categories, the approver is a project
  maintainer.

### Audit trail

- The label application is itself a GitHub event; that is the
  primary audit record.
- The approver's PR comment is the human-readable rationale.
- A follow-up entry is added to
  `.scratch/merge-gate/bypass-log.md` in the plan repo within one
  business day: PR link, approver, category, follow-up issue link.

src/web/CONTEXT.md:12:The Roster **Player** selected for the current Session, exposed to the user as "Playing against: Magnus Carlsen". Realized in the wire as the `adapter_id` sent on every `/move` request. From the user's perspective the Opponent is "the Player I face"; from the Engine's perspective it is "which Adapter is active for this request". Switching Opponents during a Session is not supported in v1 — selection is made at Session start and persists until a new Session.
src/web/CONTEXT.md:28:- A **Session** is constituted by one **Opponent** (an Adapter), one human color choice, and a growing `move_history` (the same UCI list the Engine receives on every `/move` request). When a **Terminal state** is reached, the Session ends.
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:21:2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's "same Position" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:34:- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.
src/engine/CONTEXT.md:42:- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked "is the Session over?", only "given this Position and history, what is your Move?". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

from playlike_engine.api import app

STARTING_FEN = chess.STARTING_FEN
MID_GAME_FEN = "r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/2N2N2/PPPP1PPP/R1BQKB1R w KQkq - 4 4"
CHECKMATE_FEN = "rnb1kbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/6Pq/5P2/PPPPP2P/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 1 3"
INVALID_FEN_STR = "not a fen"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client() -> TestClient:
    return TestClient(app)


def test_starting_position_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": STARTING_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = response.json()
    assert set(body.keys()) == {"move_uci"}
    board = chess.Board(STARTING_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(body["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_mid_game_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": MID_GAME_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    board = chess.Board(MID_GAME_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(response.json()["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_invalid_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": INVALID_FEN_STR})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_checkmate_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": CHECKMATE_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_extra_field_rejected_by_strict_schema(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post(
        "/move",
        json={"fen": STARTING_FEN, "adapter_id": "magnus"},
    )
    assert response.status_code == 422
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

The gate is the pre-merge phase of the harness; the full\noperations playbook (canary suite, soft→hard promotion, validator\ndisagreement runbook, roll-back) lives at\n[`docs/merge-gate-operations.md`](merge-gate-operations.md).\n\n## Blocking rule\n\n`critical` or `high` severity Codex findings classified `uphold` by\nthe Claude validator block merge. `medium` / `low` findings are\ncommented but never block. See ADR-0005 for why this MVP runs the\nClaude validator only (Gemini concordance is deferred pending\nsoft-mode measurement data).\n\n## Severity matrix\n\n| Severity | Validator `uphold` | Validator `dismiss` (cited) | Validator `unsure` |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| critical | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\n| high     | **block**    | pass         | **block** (fail-safe) |\n| medium   | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\n| low      | comment only | comment only | comment only          |\n\n## Bypass label\n\nApply the `merge-gate-bypass` label to a PR to skip the gate. Bypass\nis the **audited bypass lane** — one human (not the PR author) must\napply the label, leave a PR comment with the reason, and add an\nentry to the plan repo's bypass log within one business day. See\noperations playbook §4 for the full bypass policy.\n\n## Soft → Hard promotion\n\nThe gate ships in **soft mode** (`harness.toml`\n`[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \"true\"`): runs, reports, never\nblocks. Flip to hard mode after meeting the quantitative gates in\noperations playbook §2 (N ≥ 10 PRs, FPR ≤ 1, human sign-off) **and**\nenabling the branch-protection rule on the check\n`merge-gate / codex-review`.\n\n## Local re-installation\n\nThe skill `/setup-merge-gate` is idempotent. Re-running it shows a\ndiff for the `[merge-gate]` section of `harness.toml` and prompts\nonce; vendored agent and skill files under `.claude/` are overwritten\nunconditionally. `--uninstall` removes the workflow, the\n`[merge-gate]` section, the vendored copies, and this document (only\nif its first-line marker is intact).\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_7","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' src/engine/CONTEXT.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Engine\n\nThe runtime that, given a Position and a Player choice, returns a Move. Loads the Base model and all Roster Adapters at startup and selects an Adapter per request.\n\n## Language\n\n**Inference Engine**:\nThe runtime module wrapping the Base model and all Roster Adapters. Exposes `(Position, adapter_id, decoding_mode) → Move (+ optional policy distribution)`. Handles legality masking, decoding-mode dispatch, and per-request Adapter selection. Distinct from the Web API layer that calls it.\n_Avoid_: bare \"model\" (overloaded — see Training CONTEXT for **Base model** vs **Adapter**)\n\n**Decoding mode**:\nHow a Move is chosen from the policy logits over legal Moves. Two modes:\n- **Greedy** — deterministic argmax over the masked logits. Used by the Evaluation Harness for the top-1 Move-match metric.\n- **Sample** — temperature-scaled softmax sampling (default temperature 0.7). Used in live gameplay so repeating an opening does not produce identical Games.\n\nOrthogonal to Player choice (any Adapter can be played in either mode) and to legality (mask applies in both).\n\n**Legality mask**:\nThe boolean tensor over the 1968-action UCI space applied before decoding to set illegal-Move logits to -∞. Computed in two stages:\n1. **Rule legality** — `python-chess` `board.legal_moves` from the FEN. Covers all chess rules whose verdict depends only on the current Position (piece movement, check, pin, en-passant, castling rights encoded in FEN).\n2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's \"same Position\" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).\n\nThe 50-move-rule clock lives in the FEN and is already handled by stage 1. 5-fold and 75-move (automatic) are detected client-side as game-over events; the Inference Engine itself does not refuse to move once they occur.\n\n_Avoid_: \"legal moves filter\" — **mask** is the canonical term.\n\n**Policy distribution**:\nThe post-mask, post-softmax distribution over the 1968 UCI actions for a given Position+Adapter+decoding-mode. Returned optionally for the comparison panel and the Evaluation Harness.\n\n## Implementation notes\n\nNot domain language, but load-bearing v1 decisions:\n\n- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.\n- Adapter selection is **in-place per request**: the worker activates the requested Adapter, runs the forward pass, returns the Move, then accepts the next request. Two simultaneous requests for different Adapters are safe because they cannot overlap on the GPU (the lock guarantees no interleaved state mutation).\n- The server is **stateless per request**. Game state lives in the browser (`chess.js`). The same Position with the same Adapter under greedy decoding returns the same Move every time; under sampling, varied.\n- Throughput ceiling ≈ 7–10 moves/sec on the 3080. Practical concurrent active-user limit ≈ 25 before PRD User Story #5's \"couple of seconds\" budget starts breaking; beyond ~35 the request queue grows unboundedly. v1 has no rate limiting — this cliff is accepted as the scale of a portfolio demo. Batched inference, multi-GPU, and explicit backpressure are deliberate v2 levers (see PRD Out of Scope).\n\n## Relationships\n\n- **Training → Engine**: The Inference Engine consumes two artifact families produced by Training — one Base model checkpoint and one Adapter file per Roster Player. Engine never re-trains or modifies these artifacts.\n- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked \"is the Session over?\", only \"given this Position and history, what is your Move?\". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.\n- **Engine ⊥ Stockfish**: The Engine is not in the Stockfish comparison path. Stockfish runs client-side via `stockfish.wasm`. Engine and Stockfish are independent move sources that the Web context renders side by side.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_8","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' docs/merge-gate-operations.md\"","aggregated_output":"<!-- generated by /setup-merge-gate v1; do not edit by hand -->\n\n# Merge gate — operations playbook\n\nThis document is the human-facing playbook for the **Merge gate** —\nthe **Gate** for the pre-merge phase of the **Harness** (see\n`CONTEXT-MAP.md`). The gate runs Codex's adversarial review as the\nprimary reviewer and a Claude validator (MVP) that classifies each\nfinding against project context before the gate decides to block.\nThe MVP runs a single Claude validator per\n[ADR-0005](adr/0005-claude-only-validator-mvp-gemini-deferred.md);\na second validator returns once issue #10's soft-mode measurement\ndata is in.\n\nThe **Setup skill** (`/setup-merge-gate`) installs the gate's\nartifacts; it is not itself a gate. This playbook describes how to\n*operate* the installed gate.\n\nGlossary terms (`Harness`, `Gate`, `Audited bypass lane`,\n`Oracle weakening`) are defined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md` — do not redefine\nthem here; link or quote.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Operational phases at a glance\n\n| Phase | Branch protection | Gate behaviour | Typical use |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Soft** | Not required | Runs, posts findings, **never blocks** | Data-collection — every PR is a measurement |\n| **Hard** | Required check | Blocks merge on `critical` or `high` ∩ `uphold` | Steady state — gate is trusted |\n\nMode is recorded in `harness.toml` under `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default`\n(string boolean: `\"true\"` = soft, `\"false\"` = hard). The flip from\n`\"true\"` to `\"false\"` is HITL and is governed by §2 below.\n\nThe gate's check name is stable — `merge-gate / codex-review` — so\nbranch-protection references do not need updating across mode flips.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Soft → Hard promotion checklist\n\nThe flip from `soft` to `hard` is the single moment the gate's\nauthority changes. Treat it as a release, not a tweak. All items\nmust be checked, and the human sign-off line must be filled in.\n\n### Quantitative gates\n\n- [ ] **N ≥ 10 non-canary PRs** measured under soft mode (canary\n      PRs are excluded — they are deliberate defect injections, not\n      organic traffic).\n- [ ] **False-positive rate ≤ 1** across the N=10 set, where a false\n      positive is *validator said block, human said \"I would not have\n      wanted this blocked\"*.\n- [ ] **False-negative rate recorded** — *validator said pass, human\n      said \"I would have wanted this blocked\"*. A high FNR is **not**\n      a blocker for the soft→hard flip (the gate's purpose is to\n      cut false negatives over time), but it must be written down so\n      the next iteration can target it.\n\n### Data layout\n\nEach PR's soft-mode data lives in the project's plan repo under\n`.scratch/merge-gate/runs/<pr-number>.md` and records:\n\n- Codex JSON output (artefact link or inline)\n- Claude verdict\n- Final decision the gate would have made\n- Human verdict: would I have wanted this blocked? (yes / no / unsure)\n- One-line rationale if validator and human diverged\n\nA second validator's verdict slot will be added to this layout when\nissue #10's measurement motivates re-introducing it.\n\n### Qualitative review\n\n- [ ] Skim every entry. Look for *patterns* in the false positives\n      (e.g. \"Codex keeps flagging our intentional retry-without-cap\n      because AGENTS.md does not state the upstream timeout\n      contract\") rather than treating each as isolated noise. A\n      pattern is a signal to tighten the validator's project\n      references (§5), not to lower the bar.\n- [ ] Confirm the **Audited bypass lane** was never silently used in\n      this window — every `merge-gate-bypass` label application\n      should appear in §4's audit log.\n\n### Human sign-off\n\n> Signed off by: `<name>`\n> Date: `<YYYY-MM-DD>`\n> N = `<count>` PRs · FPR = `<count>/<N>`\n> Flipping `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default` from `\"true\"` to `\"false\"` and enabling the\n> branch-protection rule on `merge-gate / codex-review`.\n\n### After the flip\n\n- Update `harness.toml`: `[merge-gate].soft_mode_default = \"false\"`.\n- Enable the branch-protection rule (the `/setup-merge-gate` install\n  output printed the exact rule text — keep it in the install log).\n- Announce the flip in whatever channel the team uses; the first\n  organic block under hard mode is the moment people learn the gate\n  is real.\n\nIf any quantitative gate is unmet: **do not flip**. Open or update\nthe soft→hard tracking issue with what is missing and which upstream\nissue is the fix path (tighten validator prompt, expand AGENTS.md\ncontext, etc.).\n\n---\n\n## 3. Canary-PR guide\n\nCanary PRs are deliberate, scoped defect injections on throwaway\nbranches — they exercise the gate against synthetic defects in known\nrisk categories, so the gate's behaviour can be measured against a\nknown answer key.\n\n### What to canary\n\nAt minimum, one PR per category Codex's adversarial-review prompt\nprioritises:\n\n| Category | Example defect | Expected severity | Expected verdict |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| auth / authz boundary | Drop a role check on an admin endpoint | `critical` | `should-block-critical` |\n| silent data loss / missing rollback | Remove a transaction wrapper around a multi-write path | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\n| race / version-skew | Drop an optimistic-lock check on a counter update | `high` | `should-block-critical` |\n| observability gap | Replace a structured error log with a swallowed exception | `medium` | `should-warn-medium` |\n\n`should-block-critical` means the gate's final decision is *block*\nunder hard mode. `should-warn-medium` means the gate comments but\ndoes not block — `medium` and `low` severities are always\nnon-blocking by the gate's blocking rule.\n\n### Operating canaries\n\n- Each canary's brief lives at\n  `.scratch/merge-gate-canary/<NN>.md` and states injected defect,\n  category, expected severity, expected validator verdict.\n- Canary branches are pushed and PRs opened as **draft**.\n- Canary PRs are **labelled `canary`** so they are excluded from the\n  soft-mode FPR measurement in §2 (they are not organic traffic).\n- Canary branches are **closed, never merged**.\n\n### Interpreting a Codex false positive on a canary\n\nA \"Codex false positive\" on a canary means Codex flagged something\nthe brief did *not* inject. Two common causes:\n\n1. **Real latent defect.** The diff genuinely contains a problem\n   unrelated to the injection. Record it as a found bug in the\n   canary brief — that is a useful side effect, not noise.\n2. **Context starvation.** Codex flagged code that looks risky in\n   isolation but is safe given project conventions that AGENTS.md\n   does not yet spell out. This is the validator's job to dismiss\n   with a citation (γ Strict — see §5). If the validator also fails\n   to dismiss, treat it as a *project-context gap*: open an issue per\n   §4 tagged `merge-gate-fp` with a pointer to the AGENTS.md /\n   `CONTEXT-MAP.md` text that should have made the dismissal possible.\n\n### Interpreting a missed canary\n\nIf a canary's expected severity is `critical` but Codex returns\n`medium`, or returns nothing, record it in the brief with a short\nhypothesis (e.g. \"Codex missed the rollback case — likely because\nthe diff did not include the migration file\"). Missed canaries are\nthe input for tightening the canary suite and/or the Codex prompt\nin a later iteration — they are not blockers for soft→hard.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Audited bypass lane — the `merge-gate-bypass` label\n\nThe merge gate provides an **Audited bypass lane** in the sense\ndefined in `CONTEXT-MAP.md`: an acknowledged, recorded way to pass the\ngate without satisfying it. The lane exists so emergencies are not\nforced through the silent-circumvention door. It is **not** a\nshortcut for routine merges.\n\n### When the label is allowed\n\n- **Production incident hotfix** where the cost of waiting for the\n  gate exceeds the cost of a missed finding, and the fix is small\n  enough for a human to review end-to-end.\n- **Confirmed Codex false positive** that the validator could not\n  dismiss with a citation, where the team has already opened a\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue per §5 and is unwilling to block the PR on\n  the fix.\n- **Infrastructure / docs-only PR** that should have hit the\n  `docs-only` path glob but did not (treat the missed glob as a\n  bug — fix the workflow's glob in a follow-up).\n\nThe label is **never** appropriate for:\n\n- \"The gate is slow today\" — that is a §7 cost/latency issue, file\n  it there.\n- \"I disagree with the finding\" without a written argument — open a\n  `merge-gate-fp` issue and resolve through §5, not the bypass lane.\n- Avoiding the validator `unsure` runbook in §6 — bypass is the\n  lane of last resort, not the default for `unsure`.\n\n### Approval\n\n- **Single approver model.** One human who is not the PR author must\n  apply the `merge-gate-bypass` label and leave a PR comment with\n  the reason (one of the three categories above) and a link to any\n  follow-up issue.\n- For production-incident hotfixes, the approver is the on-call;\n  for the other two categories, the approver is a project\n  maintainer.\n\n### Audit trail\n\n- The label application is itself a GitHub event; that is the\n  primary audit record.\n- The approver's PR comment is the human-readable rationale.\n- A follow-up entry is added to\n  `.scratch/merge-gate/bypass-log.md` in the plan repo within one\n  business day: PR link, approver, category, follow-up issue link.\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

This keeps the board state explicit (avoiding the implicit-board, long-context problem of (α)), makes illegal-move masking a one-line operation at the policy head, and aligns with LLM tooling for LoRA-based per-player adapters — strengths the other two options each lack.\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:15:The input is a single FEN per request: no move history, no prior board states. Accepted because (a) at 9M params / 10GB VRAM the model cannot meaningfully learn long-horizon plans even if history were available, (b) adding history would multiply sequence length ~6–8× and force the model to shrink, and (c) per-Player archives are small (~10K Games), so same-FEN generalization across Games matters more than path-conditioning.\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:25:Within the macro decision above (Transformer over canonical FEN tokens, policy head over 1968 UCI actions, ~9M parameters), the *sub-architectural* details are deliberately left open at the foundation level: layer count, hidden dim, head count, positional-encoding scheme (learned absolute vs sinusoidal vs RoPE vs ALiBi vs 2D-board-aware), LayerNorm placement (pre-LN vs post-LN), MLP activation (GELU vs SwiGLU vs ReLU²), attention kernel (vanilla MHA vs `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` vs FlashAttention 2), mixed-precision dtype, dropout, init, tied embeddings. None of these leak past the model module — the external contract is just `(int tensor [B, ~80]) → logits [B, 1968]` — so deferring costs nothing downstream and decisions made after seeing real corpus statistics and 3080 forward-pass measurements are strictly better-informed than ones made at foundation time.\n./docs/adr/0001-transformer-fen-policy-head.md:27:**Encoder-only with a single readout vector → linear projection** is locked even though the rest is deferred: it bounds the forward graph (no causal mask over the FEN tokens, no autoregressive decoding loop) so downstream modules (Inference Engine, legality mask application, decoding modes) can be specified concretely now without waiting on sub-architecture decisions.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

.scratch/merge-gate-canary/03-artefacts/run-2/codex-review.json:16:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_7","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,260p' src/engine/playlike_engine/api.py"","aggregated_output":""""FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.

Phase 1 (random-move tracer): exposes POST /move that returns a uniformly
random legal move for the supplied FEN. No ML, no adapters.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import logging
import random
import string

import chess
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict

from playlike_engine.random_move import NoLegalMovesError, pick_random_legal_move

app = FastAPI(title="Playlike Chess Engine", version="0.0.1")
log = logging.getLogger("playlike_engine.api")


def _new_engine_session_token() -> str:
    """Per-request session token logged for client-side request correlation."""
    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits
    return "".join(random.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(16))


class MoveRequest(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    fen: str


class MoveResponse(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    move_uci: str


@app.post("/move", response_model=MoveResponse)
def move(req: MoveRequest) -> MoveResponse:
    token = _new_engine_session_token()
    log.info("engine session token: %s", token)
    try:
        board = chess.Board(req.fen)
    except ValueError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=f"invalid FEN: {exc}") from exc
    try:
        chosen = pick_random_legal_move(board)
    except NoLegalMovesError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=str(exc)) from exc
    return MoveResponse(move_uci=chosen.uci())
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

from __future__ import annotations

import chess
import pytest
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

from playlike_engine.api import app

STARTING_FEN = chess.STARTING_FEN
MID_GAME_FEN = "r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/2N2N2/PPPP1PPP/R1BQKB1R w KQkq - 4 4"
CHECKMATE_FEN = "rnb1kbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/6Pq/5P2/PPPPP2P/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 1 3"
INVALID_FEN_STR = "not a fen"


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def client() -> TestClient:
    return TestClient(app)


def test_starting_position_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": STARTING_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = response.json()
    assert set(body.keys()) == {"move_uci"}
    board = chess.Board(STARTING_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(body["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_mid_game_returns_legal_uci(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": MID_GAME_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 200
    board = chess.Board(MID_GAME_FEN)
    assert chess.Move.from_uci(response.json()["move_uci"]) in board.legal_moves


def test_invalid_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": INVALID_FEN_STR})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_checkmate_fen_returns_422(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post("/move", json={"fen": CHECKMATE_FEN})
    assert response.status_code == 422


def test_extra_field_rejected_by_strict_schema(client: TestClient) -> None:
    response = client.post(
        "/move",
        json={"fen": STARTING_FEN, "adapter_id": "magnus"},
    )
    assert response.status_code == 422
","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

 """FastAPI app for the Playlike Chess engine.

+import logging

 import chess

 app = FastAPI(title="Playlike Chess Engine", version="0.0.1")
+log = logging.getLogger("playlike_engine.api")

+
+def _new_engine_session_token() -> str:
+    """Per-request session token logged for client-side request correlation."""
+    alphabet = string.ascii_letters + string.digits
+    return "".join(random.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(16))
 
 
 class MoveRequest(BaseModel):
     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
     fen: str
 
 
 class MoveResponse(BaseModel):
     model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
     move_uci: str
 
 
 @app.post("/move", response_model=MoveResponse)
 def move(req: MoveRequest) -> MoveResponse:
+    token = _new_engine_session_token()

     try:

     except NoLegalMovesError as exc:
         raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=str(exc)) from exc
     return MoveResponse(move_uci=chosen.uci())

./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:21:2. **History-aware legality** — kills moves that would cause threefold repetition. Computed from the `move_history` (UCI moves from the Game's first move) that the Web context attaches to every `/move` request: the Engine replays the moves on a `python-chess` `Board`, then for each candidate Move pushes it and calls `board.is_repetition(3)`. Delegating to `python-chess` is deliberate — FIDE's "same Position" definition has subtle ep/castling/clock semantics that are easy to get wrong with manual FEN equality. The model is Markov over FEN, so without this stage the Inference Engine would have no protection against accidentally drawing a winning Position by repeating it (see ADR-0001 Markov section).
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:34:- The Inference Engine runs as a **single FastAPI worker process** on one GPU. Base + 5 Adapters share one VRAM allocation (≈52MB in FP16). All `/move` requests serialize through a GPU forward lock — concurrent requests are queued, not parallel.
./src/engine/CONTEXT.md:42:- **Engine ↔ Web**: Web calls the Engine via `POST /move` (FEN + adapter_id + move_history → Move) and `WebSocket /play`. Engine returns one legal Move per call. Web owns Session storage — the source of truth for the running Session lives in the browser's `chess.js` — but attaches the full UCI `move_history` to every request so the Engine's history-aware legality mask works without server-side state. Terminal-state detection and draw claiming remain Web-side; the Engine is never asked "is the Session over?", only "given this Position and history, what is your Move?". See `src/web/CONTEXT.md` for the Session, Opponent, and Terminal-state vocabulary.
./src/web/CONTEXT.md:12:The Roster **Player** selected for the current Session, exposed to the user as "Playing against: Magnus Carlsen". Realized in the wire as the `adapter_id` sent on every `/move` request. From the user's perspective the Opponent is "the Player I face"; from the Engine's perspective it is "which Adapter is active for this request". Switching Opponents during a Session is not supported in v1 — selection is made at Session start and persists until a new Session.
./src/web/CONTEXT.md:28:- A **Session** is constituted by one **Opponent** (an Adapter), one human color choice, and a growing `move_history` (the same UCI list the Engine receives on every `/move` request). When a **Terminal state** is reached, the Session ends.

item.text: {"verdict":"needs-attention","summary":"The diff removes the rollback that keeps chess.js, React FEN, and history synchronized after a failed engine request. This is a blocking state-corruption regression.","findings":[{"severity":"high","title":"Engine-error rollback leaves chess.js advanced after removing undo","body":"When postMove rejects for a non-terminal user move, the hook still advertises this path as a rollback and slices the last history entry, but it no longer undoes the already-applied user move in chessRef. setFen(chessRef.current.fen()) now publishes the advanced board instead of the pre-move board, while history removes the user's UCI move and error clears awaiting. The next move will be made from a position the engine rejected and a move_history that no longer matches the board, breaking the Web/Engine session contract and causing persistent client state corruption after any transient /move failure or 422 that is not terminal.","file":"src/web/app/hooks/useChessSession.ts","line_start":82,"line_end":83,"confidence":0.99,"recommendation":"Restore chessRef.current.undo() before setFen in the catch rollback path, and add a hook test that mocks a rejected /move after a legal user move and asserts FEN and history both return to the starting state."}],"next_steps":["Restore the missing chessRef.current.undo() in the requestEngineMove catch rollback branch.","Add targeted regression coverage for non-terminal engine failure rollback."]}
